content:
The 3-phase modulating waveforms $\left(v_a(t), v_b(t), v_c(t)\right)$, used in sinusoidal PWM in a voltage source inverter (VSI) are

$$ \begin{aligned} & v_a(t)=0.8 \sin (\omega t) \vee \\ & v_b(t)=0.8 \sin \left(\omega t-\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \vee \\ & v_c(t)=0.8 \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{2 \pi}{3}\right) \vee \end{aligned} $$

where $\omega=2 \pi \times 40 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ is the fundamental frequency. The modulating waveforms are compared with a 10 kHz triangular carrier whose magnitude varies between +1 and -1 . The VSI has a DC link voltage of 600 V and feeds a star connected motor. The per phase fundamental RMS motor voltage in volts is closest to
explanation:
In a three-phase voltage source inverter (VSI) using sinusoidal PWM, the modulating waveforms for each phase are given by:

$ v_a(t) = 0.8 \sin(\omega t) $

$ v_b(t) = 0.8 \sin\left(\omega t - \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) $

$ v_c(t) = 0.8 \sin\left(\omega t + \frac{2\pi}{3}\right) $

The fundamental frequency is $\omega = 2\pi \times 40$ rad/s. These modulating waveforms are compared with a 10 kHz triangular carrier wave whose amplitude ranges from +1 to -1. The VSI is powered by a DC link voltage of 600 V and supplies a motor connected in a star configuration.

To find the fundamental RMS voltage per phase ($V_{Ph1 \, \text{RMS}}$), follow these steps:

Calculation Steps:

Amplitude Ratio Calculation:

Modulating wave amplitude, $A_M = 0.8 \, \text{V}$

Carrier wave amplitude, $A_C = 1 \, \text{V}$

Modulation index, $M_A = \frac{A_M}{A_C} = \frac{0.8}{1} = 0.8$

Line-to-Line Voltage Calculation:

Peak line-to-line voltage:

$ \hat{V}_{L1} = \sqrt{3} \cdot M_A \cdot \frac{V_S}{2} $

Fundamental RMS Line Voltage:

$ V_{L1}^{\text{RMS}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot M_A \cdot V_S $

Substituting the known values:

$ = 0.612 \times 0.8 \times 600 = 293.938 \, \text{V} $

Per Phase RMS Voltage:

Converting line-to-line RMS voltage to per phase RMS:

$ V_{Ph1 \, \text{RMS}} = \frac{V_{L1}^{\text{RMS}}}{\sqrt{3}} $

Calculation:

$ = \frac{293.938}{\sqrt{3}} = 169.7 \, \text{V} $

Therefore, the per phase fundamental RMS motor voltage is approximately 169.7 V.

content: A single-phase full bridge voltage source inverter (VSI) feeds a purely inductive load. The inverter output voltage is a square wave in 180° conduction mode. The fundamental frequency of the output voltage is 50 Hz. If the DC input voltage of the inverter is 100 V and the value of the load inductance is 20 mH, the peak-to-peak load current in amperes is ________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).

Consider an ideal full-bridge single-phase DC-AC inverter with a DC bus voltage magnitude of 1000 V. The inverter output voltage v(t) shown below, is obtained when diagonal switches of the inverter are switched with 50% duty cycle. The inverter feeds a load with a sinusoidal current given by, $$i(t) = 10\sin \left( {\omega t - {\pi \over 3}} \right)A$$, where $$\omega = {{2\pi } \over T}$$. The active power, in watts, delivered to the load is __________. (round off to nearest integer).
explanation:
P0 = P01

= V01 . I0r . cos(60$$^\circ$$)

$$ = \left( {{{2\sqrt 2 } \over \pi }\,.\,(1000)} \right)\,.\,\left( {{{10} \over {\sqrt 2 }}} \right) \times (0.5)$$

$$ \simeq $$ 3182 W

content: A 3-phase grid-connected voltage source converter with DC link voltage of 1000 V is switched using sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) technique. If the rid phase current is 10 A and the 3-phase complex power supplied by the converter is given by ($$-$$4000 $$-$$ j3000) VA, then the modulation index used in sinusoidal PWM is _________. (round off to two decimal places).
explanation:
$$S = ( - 4000 - j3000)\,VA$$

$${P_0} = \sqrt 3 \,.\,{V_{L1}}\,.\,{I_{0r}}\,.\,(pF)$$

$$4000 = \sqrt 3 \,.\,{V_{L1}}\,.\,10 \times \left( {{{4000} \over {\sqrt {{{4000}^2} + {{3000}^2}} }}} \right)$$

$${V_{L1\,(rms)}} = 288.675\,V$$

3-$$\phi$$ VSI $$-$$ SPWM, $${M_A} \le 1$$

$${\widehat V_{L1}} = \sqrt 3 \,.\,{M_A}\,.\,{{{V_s}} \over 2}$$

$${V_{L1\,(rms)}} = {M_A}\left( {{{\sqrt 3 } \over {2\sqrt 2 }} \times 1000} \right)$$

$$288.675 = {M_A}\left( {{{\sqrt 3 } \over {2\sqrt 2 }} \times 1000} \right)$$

$${M_A} = 0.47$$

content:
A single-phase full bridge inverter fed by a 325 V DC produces a symmetric quasi-square waveform across " $a b$ " as shown. To achieve a modulation index of 0.8 , the angle $\theta$ expressed in degrees should be $\_\_\_\_$ . (Round off to 2 decimal places)

(Modulation index is defined as the ratio of the peak of the fundamental component of $V_{d b}$ to the applied DC value)
explanation:
Fourier series expression for single pulse width modulation is given by,

$$ V_0(n t)=\sum\limits_{n=1,3,5}^{\infty} \frac{4 V_s}{n \pi} \sin (n d) \sin \left(\frac{n \pi}{2}\right) \sin n \omega t $$

Fundamental component of single pulse width modulation is given by,

$$ V_{0_1}(t)=\frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sin (d) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Peak amplitude of fundamental component of single pulse with a modulation index of 0.8 is

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{0_1}=m V_s \\ & V_{0_1}=0.8 V_s\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) \end{aligned} $$

Comparing equation (i) and (ii),

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{4 V_s}{\pi} \sin (d)=0.8 V_s \\ & \sin d=0.62832 \end{aligned} $$

Therefore, $\quad \theta=\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-d\right)=51.07^{\circ}$

Hence, the correct answer is 51.07 .
